plainMeaning: We sing praise to the victorious Jina — the worshipful, all-knowing, all-seeing ford-maker who is perfected, awakened, eternal, and the supreme reality.
recitation: arihaṁtaṁ bhagavaṁtaṁ, savvanuṁ savvadaṁsi titthayaraṁ | siddhaṁ buddhaṁ niccaṁ, paramapayatthaṁ jiṇaṁ thuṇimo ||

wordByWord.gloss: the Arihanta (destroyer of inner foes) — accusative, object of praise
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit arihaṁta = Skt. arhat / ari-hanta: ari ('enemy', i.e. the karmas/passions) + √han ('to destroy'); the embodied omniscient one.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative -ṁ throughout marks every epithet as object of thuṇimo.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhagavaṁta = Skt. bhagavant, 'possessing bhaga' (glory, majesty, fortune); honorific epithet, here in apposition to arihaṁtaṁ.
Prakrit savvaṇṇū = Skt. sarvajña: savva ('all') + jña/ṇṇū ('knower'); the book's reading savvanuṁ is a softened orthographic variant of savvaṇṇū.
wordByWord.notes: Denotes kevala-jñāna (perfect knowledge). Book spelling preserved verbatim (not normalised to savvaṇṇū).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savvadaṁsi = Skt. sarvadarśin: savva ('all') + darśin ('seer'); paired with savvanuṁ as the standard kevala-jñāna + kevala-darśana dyad.
Prakrit titthayara = Skt. tīrthaṅkara: tittha ('ford/crossing') + kara ('maker').
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term — used, not re-defined here.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Skt. siddha, from √sidh ('to be accomplished, perfected'); the soul freed of all karma.
wordByWord.notes: Shifts the praise from the embodied (arhat) to the liberated (siddha) state within one breath.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Skt. buddha, past participle of √budh ('to awaken, know'); 'one who has awakened to reality'.
wordByWord.notes: In Jain usage denotes full spiritual awakening, not the Buddhist title.

wordByWord.gloss: the eternal / the everlasting one (or adverbially, 'forever')
wordByWord.notes: Can read as an epithet of the siddha-state (eternal) or adverbially with thuṇimo ('we praise forever').

wordByWord.gloss: the supreme reality / one established in the highest state
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit parama-payattha: parama ('highest, supreme') + payattha = Skt. padārtha ('reality, entity') or pada-stha ('established in the [highest] state'); both readings are current.
wordByWord.notes: See notes: the compound supports 'supreme Tattva' and 'established in the supreme abode (siddha-loka)' alike.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṇa = Skt. jina, from √ji ('to conquer'); conqueror of rāga, dveṣa and moha.
wordByWord.notes: The grammatical head all preceding epithets qualify.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit thuṇimo, 1st person plural of thuṇa- (Skt. √stu, stumaḥ 'we praise'); Skt. st- → Prakrit th-.
wordByWord.notes: Plural 'we' frames the hymn as a communal act of praise; the sole finite verb governing the verse.

literalTranslation: The Arihanta, the Blessed One, the all-knowing, the all-seeing, the Tīrthaṅkara, the Perfected (Siddha), the Awakened, the Eternal, the supreme reality — the Jina — we praise.
idiomaticTranslation: We lift our praise to the Jina: worshipful Arihanta, all-knowing and all-seeing ford-maker, perfected and awakened, eternal, the very Supreme Reality.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The hymn opens not with a place but with the One who makes every place holy. The whole verse is a single garland of accusative epithets strung on one finite verb, thuṇimo ('we praise') — and the deliberate craft lies in which epithets are chosen and in what order. Arihaṁtaṁ–bhagavaṁtaṁ–titthayaraṁ name the Jina in his embodied, teaching aspect: the foe-destroyer still in the body, worshipful, re-founding the ford across saṁsāra. Sandwiched between them, savvanuṁ savvadaṁsi name the twin marks that prove that state — sarvajña and sarvadarśin, perfect knowing and perfect seeing, the kevala-jñāna/kevala-darśana dyad that arises the instant the four ghātī-karmas fall. The second line then steps beyond the body: siddhaṁ buddhaṁ niccaṁ — perfected, awakened, eternal — is the liberated, deathless aspect, the soul in siddha-hood. So in two lines the verse fuses the two highest Parameṣṭhin states, arhat and siddha, onto one object, and seals them with paramapayatthaṁ jiṇaṁ — the Jina who is the 'supreme reality' itself. The movement is from attribute to essence: many epithets, one Jina. By making the verb plural ('we'), the reciter is enrolled into a chorus, and the hymn's long pilgrimage is launched from its only true source — the Victor in whom knowing, seeing, perfection and eternity are one.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compresses the Jain account of liberation into an epithet-sequence. Sarvajña + sarvadarśin (savvanuṁ savvadaṁsi) are not loose synonyms but the two simultaneous faculties — infinite knowledge and infinite perception — that define omniscience once the knowledge- and perception-obscuring karmas are annihilated. The arhat/siddha pairing encodes the doctrine that the Arihanta (omniscient yet embodied) and the Siddha (bodiless, karma-free) are successive states of the same victor, the very distinction drawn in the first two lines of the Navkāra. Niccaṁ asserts the eternality of the liberated soul; paramapayatthaṁ affirms the Jina as the highest padārtha/pada — the supreme reality and the supreme abode toward which the entire 108-tīrtha pilgrimage points.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Before naming a single shrine, the reciter fixes the object of all pilgrimage: not the stone or the site but the Jina's qualities. Chanting this opening trains attention to begin every devotion from essence, not geography — so that the journey through 108 places that follows is felt as one continuous bow to the same omniscient Victor.

crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses for savvaṇṇū/savvadaṁsi, thuṇa-, arihaṁta, jiṇa

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)

notes: Book reading savvanuṁ (સવ્વનું) is preserved verbatim; it is an orthographic softening of the expected savvaṇṇū (sarvajña) and is read as 'omniscient'. paramapayatthaṁ is genuinely two-valued: parama-padārtha ('supreme reality/Tattva') and parama-pada-stha ('established in the supreme abode'); both are doctrinally apt and the verse likely intends the resonance. niccaṁ (nitya) may be taken as an epithet ('the eternal') or adverbially with thuṇimo ('we praise constantly'); the epithet reading is preferred here for parallelism with siddhaṁ/buddhaṁ. buddhaṁ is the Jain 'awakened', not a Buddhist reference. No verified critical apparatus for this verse's variant readings was located beyond the source edition.
